questions.question: Define a queue data structure and explain the FIFO principle with a real-world example.
keyPoints: Queue is a linear data structure that follows First-In-First-Out (FIFO) principle
The first element inserted is the first element to be removed
Real-world example: people waiting in a ticket counter line or cars at a toll booth
Elements are added at the rear and removed from the front

questions.question: Explain the problem of memory wastage in a simple linear queue implemented using an array. How is this problem overcome in a circular queue?
keyPoints: In linear queue, front and rear indices move only in forward direction
After multiple dequeue operations, empty spaces are created at the beginning of the array
When rear reaches MAX-1, queue is reported full despite available space at front indices
This limitation is overcome by implementing a Circular Queue
In circular queue, rear wraps around to the beginning using modulo arithmetic
Circular queue reuses empty slots by connecting the last position to the first

questions.question: Write C code to implement enqueue and dequeue operations on a queue using an array. Include proper overflow and underflow condition checks.
keyPoints: Enqueue: Check if queue is full (rear == MAX-1), increment rear and insert element
For first element, set front = 0 when performing enqueue
Dequeue: Check if queue is empty (front == -1 || front > rear), retrieve and return queue[front]
Increment front after dequeue; reset front and rear to -1 when last element is removed
Both operations have O(1) time complexity

questions.question: List and explain any four applications of queues in computer science.
keyPoints: CPU Scheduling: Operating systems use queues to manage processes waiting for CPU time
Breadth-First Search (BFS): Queue is used to traverse graphs level by level
Print Spooling: Documents are queued and printed in order of arrival
I/O Buffering: Queues buffer data between fast and slow devices

questions.question: Trace the queue operations: enqueue(10), enqueue(20), enqueue(30), dequeue(), dequeue(), enqueue(40). Show the array contents, front and rear indices after each operation.
keyPoints: enqueue(10): queue=[10,-,-], front=0, rear=0
enqueue(20): queue=[10,20,-], front=0, rear=1
enqueue(30): queue=[10,20,30], front=0, rear=2
dequeue(): returns 10, queue=[-,20,30], front=1, rear=2
dequeue(): returns 20, queue=[-,-,30], front=2, rear=2
enqueue(40): queue=[-,-,30,40], front=2, rear=3 (overflow in linear queue if MAX=3)

questions.question: Compare and contrast queue and stack data structures with respect to their ordering principle, insertion and deletion points, and applications.
keyPoints: Queue follows FIFO (First-In-First-Out); Stack follows LIFO (Last-In-First-Out)
Queue: insertion at rear (enqueue), deletion from front (dequeue)
Stack: insertion and deletion both at top (push and pop)
Queue applications: CPU scheduling, BFS, print spooling, buffers
Stack applications: DFS, expression parsing, recursive function calls, undo operations
